feedback.text: Greetings. I firmly believe that achieving fairness in the exchange of digital goods is not possible without legal regulations governing the licensing of various products. These regulations are necessary and long overdue. Currently, many companies attempt to circumvent existing customer rights and protections by offering "licenses to access" specific services or digital products such as movies, books, software, and games. This practice is both unfair and unjust. Beyond entertainment, most jobs require software for efficient performance. These tools are essential in todays world. They MUST function as intended and should not be subject to artificial expiration due to the whims of monopolistic companies, which leads to increased costs and diminished quality for everyone in society. Most companies have moved away from selling perpetual licenses, meaning customers no longer legally own the digital products they purchase. We should have never let this happen. This shift has resulted in a decline in software quality while prices have soared. Legally, software companies should be required to offer perpetual licenses for their products at a price equal to twelve times the monthly subscription fee (a yearly fee), along with a two-year commitment to provide free bug fixes and updates. Such regulations would compel companies to genuinely enhance their products to boost sales rather than relying on monopolistic practices that could render essential tools inoperable without timely payments. Surely we do not wish to be enslaved to our corporate overlords like in some dystopian nightmare, because this is exactly where the current corporate trends are leading us. We need to stop them while we still can. Furthermore, new legislation must be introduced regarding warranties, refunds, and reimbursements for software that is defective or does not function as described in its documentation. This would make the process of requesting refunds more straightforward and enforceable for customers. Software serves as a fundamental tool in modern society, and it should be treated accordingly. When I purchase a power tool that fails to perform as promised, I can easily approach the retailer for a refund or repair. However, with software, we often receive fixes for missing or broken features only through new versions that come at an additional cost. This needs to be stopped. Regarding entertainment, such as films and video games: Video games should always be sold as perpetual licenses owned by the customer, allowing for ownership transfer in cases like inheritance (though resale of videogames and movies should be restricted to avoid complications in tracking who actually owns the license now and to not damage the revenue of artists whose work can easily be stolen). Companies must provide offline game and update installers to ensure customers can secure their digital game copies. For multiplayer, MMO, or live service games, companies should be mandated to release server files, enabling customers to host their own servers if the original creators no longer support the services. In the case of streaming services, they should automatically cease charging customers for months in which the services are not utilized. This requirement would pressure large companies to adapt and potentially consolidate into larger, more fairly priced services. I sincerely hope that the people of Europe, my fellow citizens, are ready to challenge the greedy practices enforced by large corporations and our collective voices will be heard, leading to a change for the better. Only together can we ever make this incredibly important step towards a better future.
